coredata.dc:title: Dual color carbon dots for simultaneous dynamic fluorescence tracking of mitochondria and lysosomes
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.

coredata.dc:creator: Li, Xinlu
Zhu, Tongtong
Quan, Xiangli
Yan, Haiyang
Du, Yuwei
Yan, Ruhong
Dong, Wen-Fei
Li, Li
coredata.dc:description: The development of fluorescent probes to monitor mitochondria and lysosomes will be helpful not only to realize the status monitoring of the organelles, but also to gain insights into their functions in both healthy and diseased states. Carbon Dots (CDs), as a nanomaterial with excellent properties, have provided new tools and methods for research in organelle imaging in recent years. In this study, we synthesized green fluorescence-emitting carbon dots (G-CDs) with mitochondria-targeting ability and red fluorescence-emitting carbon dots (R-CDs) with lysosome-targeting ability by using the same precursors. G-CDs are brightly with a quantum yield of up to 51.8 % and R-CDs have a long-wavelength emission of 611 nm. Based on the unique properties of G-CDs and R-CDs, we observed the changes of mitochondria during apoptosis and mitochondrial autophagy in HeLa cells by using G-CDs. And the dynamics of lysosomes in the inhibition of cellular autophagy and cellular necrosis were observed by utilizing the R-CDs. Furthermore, we observed human intestinal organoids samples stained by G-CDs and R-CDs, extending the relevant applications of carbon dots imaging to the organoids field, which brings forth new possibilities for research and drug development of related diseases.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Mitochondria dynamic
Lysosomes dynamic
Organoid imaging

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This research was funded by the National Key R&D Program of China (2023YFB3210300), the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 82172077, 22201298 and 62027825), the Science Foundation of the Chinese Academy of Sciences (No.2023SYHZ0037), the Natural Science Foundation of Jiangsu Province of China (BK20231207), the Innovation and Entrepreneurship Team of Jiangsu Province (JSSCTD202145), the Science Foundation of the Chinese Academy of Sciences (No.2023SYHZ0037), the Projects of Chinese Academy of Sciences (YJKYYQ20200038 and YJKYYQ20210032), and the Youth Innovation Promotion Association of CAS (2022326).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image of G-CDs. The inset in the upper right is HR-TEM image and the inset in the lower right is size distribution image. (b) TEM image of R-CDs. The inset in the upper right is HR-TEM image and the inset in the lower right is size distribution image. (c) The FTIR spectra of the G-CDs and R-CDs. (d-g) XPS spectrum, N 1 s, C 1 s and O 1 s spectra of the G-CDs. (h-k) XPS spectrum, N 1 s, C 1 s and O 1 s spectra of the R-CDs.

(a) Excitation, and emission spectra of the G-CDs. (b) Three-dimensional luminescence spectrum of the G-CDs. (c) CIE color coordinates of the G-CDs, which are (0.2668, 0.6137). (d) Excitation, and emission spectra of the R-CDs. (e) Three-dimensional luminescence spectrum of the R-CDs. (f) CIE color coordinates of the G-CDs, which are (0.5163, 0.4697).

Images of HeLa cells obtained via laser scanning confocal microscopy. (a) HeLa cells incubated with 100 μg/mL G-CDs, Mito-Tracker Red and Lyso-Tracker Red. (b) HeLa cells incubated with 100 μg/mL R-CDs, Mito-Tracker Green and Lyso-Tracker Green. (Scale bar: 20 µm. Green channel (λ = 488 nm), red channel (λ = 561 nm).
(a) and (b) Fluorescence intensity of G-CDs at different pH condition; (c) and (d) Fluorescence intensity of R-CDs at different pH condition; (e) Zeta potential at different pH conditions.
MMP of mitochondria indicated by G-CDs and TMRE in HeLa cells. The images in the Blank group show untreated cells, and the images in the CCCP group are cells that have been treated with CCCP.
CLSM of G-CDs-stained HeLa cells incubated with rapamycin for 0–20 min, as well as structural enlargements and threshold pictures of mitochondria treated by ImageJ.

(a) Fluorescence images were captured by advance staining of unfixed HeLa cells. Fluorescence images of untreated HeLa cells and stimulated with 5 mM chloroquine for 4, 8, 12 and 16 min. Two different time images were combined; (b) 0.5 % NaOH-induced cell necrosis is monitored by R-CDs.

(a-c) Intestinal organoids stained by Dapi, G-CDs and Mito-Tracker Red. (d) Bright field pictures of colorectal cancer organoids. (e) Merged images of (a) to (d). (f-j) Intestinal organoids stained by Dapi, R-CDs and Lyso-Tracker Green. (i) Bright field pictures of colorectal cancer organoids. (e) Merged images of (f) to (i). (k)-(n) 3D confocal images of intestinal-like organs. Blue, green, and red channels are fluorescence images of Dapi, G-CDs and R-CDs.
Fluorescence, bright-field and merged images of zebrafish larvae and eggs incubated with G-CDs (a) and R-CDs (b). The scale bar is 200 μm.
The synthesis of G-CDs and R-CDs and their applications in live-cell dynamic monitoring of mitochondria and lysosomes, as well as in organoid and zebrafish imaging.

head.ce:title: Dual color carbon dots for simultaneous dynamic fluorescence tracking of mitochondria and lysosomes

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Biomedical Engineering (Suzhou), Division of Life Sciences and Medicine, University of Science and Technology of China, Hefei 230026, China

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: CAS Key Laboratory of Biomedical Diagnostics, Suzhou Institute of Biomedical Engineering and Technology, Chinese Academy of Science (CAS), Suzhou 215163, China.

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Biological Sciences, Xi’an Jiaotong-Liverpool University, Suzhou 215123, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Biological Sciences, Xi’an Jiaotong-Liverpool University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Biological Sciences, Xi’an Jiaotong-Liverpool University, Suzhou 215123, China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The development of fluorescent probes to monitor mitochondria and lysosomes will be helpful not only to realize the status monitoring of the organelles, but also to gain insights into their functions in both healthy and diseased states. Carbon Dots (CDs), as a nanomaterial with excellent properties, have provided new tools and methods for research in organelle imaging in recent years. In this study, we synthesized green fluorescence-emitting carbon dots (G-CDs) with mitochondria-targeting ability and red fluorescence-emitting carbon dots (R-CDs) with lysosome-targeting ability by using the same precursors. G-CDs are brightly with a quantum yield of up to 51.8 % and R-CDs have a long-wavelength emission of 611 nm. Based on the unique properties of G-CDs and R-CDs, we observed the changes of mitochondria during apoptosis and mitochondrial autophagy in HeLa cells by using G-CDs. And the dynamics of lysosomes in the inhibition of cellular autophagy and cellular necrosis were observed by utilizing the R-CDs. Furthermore, we observed human intestinal organoids samples stained by G-CDs and R-CDs, extending the relevant applications of carbon dots imaging to the organoids field, which brings forth new possibilities for research and drug development of related diseases.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: With the development of biology, visualization of cells has become an important tool in biological research[1]. Fluorescent probes are regarded as an effective and convenient method for revealing the distribution and dynamics of biomolecules within cells[2]. Green synthetic fluorescent probes are especially necessary due to the potential negative impact of conventional probes on biological systems[3]. Carbon dots (CDs) are a class of nanoparticles of dimensions less than 10 nm with fluorescent properties, synthesized using organic compounds as precursors. Due to their unique optical characteristics, strong resistance to photobleaching, and excellent biocompatibility, carbon dots have garnered significant attention from researchers[4]. Owing to these advantages, the applications of carbon quantum dots extend beyond the engineering domain and have found widespread use in the biological field, including cellular imaging, drug delivery, biosensing, and tumor therapy[5,6].

Mitochondria and lysosomes are two critical organelles within cells. Mitochondria regulate intracellular calcium homeostasis and play a role in apoptosis by generating adenosine triphosphate (ATP) through oxidative phosphorylation[7]. While lysosomes are responsible for degrading endocytosed substances and materials of extracellular origin. As microscopic cellular structures, dysfunction of these organelles can lead to severe diseases, including Alzheimer's disease, cancer, cardiovascular diseases, and lysosomal storage disorders[8,9]. In contrast, organoids are simplified in vitro organ models composed of multiple cells, capable of more accurately replicating the overall structure and function of an organ[10,11]. This capability positions organoids as highly promising tools for disease modeling, personalized medicine, and drug screening. The study of various imaging modalities for organoids has greatly contributed to a deeper understanding of the internal structure and function of biological systems[12–14]. Thus, it is imperative to expand the toolkit for observing organoids through microscopy, including the design of reliable probes for organoid observation.

Given these properties, carbon dots are considered to be reliable fluorescent probes for the visualization of organelles as well as complex organoids in living cells. Recent studies have demonstrated that CDs can target different organelles through diverse precursor selection or intrinsic chemical properties of surface functional group modifications [15]. The multiplicity and low biotoxicity of the carbon dot targeting potential, as well as the stable optical properties, make it an excellent probe for dynamic tracing of organelles in living cells. As complex three-dimensional structures, organoids require efficient and stable fluorescent probes for long-term, multi-angle imaging[16]. The versatility of carbon dots in surface modification enables them to specifically label particular cell populations or subcellular structures within organoids. Furthermore, the nanoscale size and tunable optical properties of carbon dots allow them to penetrate deeper into organoid structures, facilitating more detailed internal imaging, thus establishing them as a powerful tool for organoid imaging[17].

In this work, we applied the same precursors to synthesize two types of carbon dots by a "bottom-up approach", which are characterized by green and red fluorescence emission for mitochondria and lysosome imaging and tracking of cellular physiological activities, respectively. 1,5-diaminoanthraquinone, vanillic acid and ethylenediamine were used as precursors, and water and ethanol were added as solvents to the reaction system to synthesize 515 nm-emitting G-CDs and 611 nm-emitting R-CDs. The G-CDs and R-CDs exhibit impressive optical properties, including bright and stable fluorescence, as well as specific mitochondrial and lysosomal targeting properties, which makes them suitable for live cell organelle imaging and dynamic tracking applications. Systematic cellular experiments reveal the application of G-CDs and R-CDs in the dynamic monitoring of apoptosis, cell necrosis and autophagy. Additionally, G-CDs and R-CDs were demonstrated out of the potential of zebrafish bioimaging and organoid imaging applications Scheme 1.

Chemicals used in the synthesis of CDs, including 1,5-diaminoanthraquinone, vanillic acid, ethylenediamine purchased from Adamas-beta, Shanghai, China, as well as anhydrous ethanol (≥99.8 %). And Metal ion solutions were all configured using reagents purchased from Aladdin Ltd. Fluorescent probes for bioimaging were purchased from Beyotime Institute of Biotechnology (Shanghai, China), including Lyso-Tracker Green/Red, Mito-Tracker Green/Red, Tetramethylrhodamine ethyl ester (TMRE) and WST-1 Cell Proliferation and Cytotoxicity Assay Kit. The medium and phosphate buffer solution for cell culture experiments obtained from Solarbio Science & Technology Co., Ltd. Zebrafish for bioimaging were obtained from Nanjing EzeRinka Biotechnology Co., Ltd.

High resolution TEM (HRTEM) and standard TEM of G-CDs and R-CDs were obtained by utilizing a transmission electron microscope with a TECNAI G20 in order to measure the morphology of the CDs. FTIR spectra were obtained using a VERTEX 70 FT-IR spectrometer

from Bruker, Germany. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was performed by a PHI Quantera II manufactured by Ulvac-Phi, Japan. A Hitachi U-3000 Scan spectrophotometer and an F97 Pro fluorescence spectrophotometer were used, respectively, for collecting experimental data for UV-Visible absorption spectroscopy and fluorescence spectroscopy. Bioimaging was conducted using Nikon A1+ and Leica TCS-SP5 confocal microscopes. Nikon A1+ and Leica TCS-SP5 confocal microscopes were used for obtaining the fluorescence images in the bioimaging section.

G-CDs were synthesized by hydrothermally treating a solution containing 0.05 g of 1,5-diaminoanthraquinone, 0.05 g of vanillic acid, and 1 mL of ethylenediamine in 30 mL of water in a 100 mL polytetrafluoroethylene reactor. Ethanol was used to replace for water in a one-pot solvent-thermal process to synthesize R-CDs. To obtain a clarified solution of CDs, the reaction product was centrifuged after it had cooled to room temperature. Following that, the resultant solution was concentrated, and dichloromethane and methanol were used as eluents in column chromatography to purify the product. G-CDs and R-CDs were eluted with dichloromethane: methanol ratios of 10:1 and 20:1, respectively. G-CDs and R-CDs were eluted with dichloromethane: methanol ratios of 10:1 and 20:1. For subsequent use, the G-CDs and R-CDs were dissolved in 10 mg/mL of ethanol and water.

HeLa cells were cultured in a controlled environment at 37°C with a 5 % CO2 atmosphere, which contained 10 % fetal bovine serum (FBS) and 1 % penicillin/streptomycin. After incubating HeLa cells in 96-well plates at 8000 cells per well for 24 h, the two carbon dots were added and incubated for 24 h to detect the toxicity of carbon dots to the cells. Cytotoxicity assay was measured using Wst-1 assay. Additionally, a Living/Dead cell double staining kit (Calcein-AM/PI) was used to determine the number of live dead cells after a 4-hour incubation of G-CDs and R-CDs.

HeLa cells were pretreated with Chlorpromazine (CPZ), Genistein (Genistein), Amiloride, and Methyl-β-cyclodextrin (MβCD) for two hours, preceding the addition of G-CDs and R-CDs, in order to study the internalization pathways of the CDs. HeLa cells were then incubated with G-CDs and R-CDs for 1 hour before being imaged using confocal microscopy for fluorescence imaging.

HeLa cells were used as model cells to assess the potential application of synthesized G-CDs and R-CDs in cell imaging. Initially, HeLa cells (at a density of 8000 cells/mL) were seeded in glass-bottomed culture dishes and cultured at 37°C for 24 hours. For the co-localization experiments, G-CDs (100 μg/mL) were injected into the culture dish and HeLa cells were incubated at 37°C for 60 min. For the co-localization studies, G-CDs (at a concentration of 100 μg/mL) were added to the culture medium, followed by further incubation of the HeLa cells at 37°C for 60 minutes. After incubation, the cells were washed three times with phosphate-buffered saline (PBS) to remove any unbound CDs. Subsequently, the cells were stained for 20 minutes using Mito-Tracker Red and Lyso-Tracker Red (each at 50 nM), and then imaged immediately. The acquisition of images was performed using a confocal laser scanning microscope. Colocalization experiments were performed by injecting R-CDs (100 μg/mL) into cells culture medium by a similar procedure described above, with the difference being the use of commercial organelle markers containing Lyso-tracker Green and Mito-racker Green for labeling.

To monitor the membrane potential changes of mitochondria during apoptosis and the dynamics of mitochondria during mitochondrial autophagy, HeLa cells were first cultured in glass-bottomed culture dishes as previously described. Cells were pre-treated with CCCP (10 mM) for 2 hours to induce apoptosis, resulting in alterations in the normal mitochondrial membrane potential. Then, G-CD (100 μg/mL) was stained at 37 °C for 60 min and fluorescence images of the cells were recorded. Cells and G-CDs were initially co-incubated at 37°C for 60 minutes. Subsequently, the cells were treated with rapamycin (5 μg/mL) to induce mitochondrial autophagy. Fluorescence images at different time points, including 0, 5, 10, and 20 minutes after the addition of rapamycin, were captured using confocal microscopy.

We performed bioimaging of zebrafish and organoids using the two carbon dots synthesized above. Zebrafish, rendered transparent throughout their bodies, were cultured in a medium supplemented with the melanin inhibitor 1-phenyl-2-thiourea for observation convenience. The experimental animals were reared in an environment where the temperature was maintained at 28.5°C. Zebrafish larvae and eggs were cultured using a medium supplemented with 100 μg mL-1 G-CDs and RCDs. Following a 20-minute treatment period, new zebrafish medium was added in substitute of the CDs solution. Subsequently, zebrafish were imaged using confocal microscopy with a 10× objective. All experiments were conducted following the administration of anesthetic MS-222 to alleviate pain in experimental animals.

Fresh tumor tissue samples were successively digested by Liberase and TrypLE Express into cells, which were collected by centrifugation. Cells were resuspended in matrix gel and inoculated in a volume of 30 μL into confocal culture dishes. Organoid-specific medium was added to the dishes to flood over the cells. The method of preparing the basal medium for human intestinal organoids is shown in the Supplementary Material. After 5 days of growth in the medium, organoid samples were used for imaging.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and characterization of G-CDs and R-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: G-CDs and R-CDs with two different fluorescence wavelength emitting carbon dots were prepared by one-step hydrothermal and ethanol solvent thermal methods. It is well known that column chromatography is widely used for the crude products of CDs in order to obtain narrow fluorescence emission spectra[18,19]. Therefore, G-CDs and R-CDs were purified by column chromatography, and some materials science characterization tools were utilized, respectively. The morphology of CDs prepared after purification was examined by transmission electron microscopy. TEM images showed that both G-CDs ( Fig. 1a) and R-CDs (Fig. 1b) were well dispersed. Calculating the particle size of 50 particles based on the TEM images, the average diameters of G-CDs and R-CDs were 2.48 nm and 3.90 nm, respectively. In order to further identify the graphite structure of the particles, the HR-TEM images were examined for Fast Fourier Transform (FFT) and Fast Fourier Inverse Transform (IFFT) results. D-spacing was calculated as 0.210 nm and 0.209 nm[20]. The above experimental results confirm the successful fabrication of R-CDs.

To further determine the structure of the carbon dots, the Raman spectra of G-CDs and R-CDs are presented in Fig. S1. The peak around 1350 cm⁻¹ corresponds to the D band, while the peak near 1580 cm⁻¹ corresponds to the G band, representing the stretching vibrations of the graphite plane. The calculated ID/IG ratio for G-CDs is 0.97, and for R-CDs, it is 0.77. This indicates a good degree of graphitization for both materials. The stronger D band in G-CDs may be attributed to a higher abundance of surface groups[21]. We have also obtained more structural information about the carbon dots by X-ray diffraction (XRD), which results are shown in Fig. S2. The data showed that the G-CDs have peaks at 20.1° and 22.8°; while the R-CDs have peaks at 25.0° and 26.7°. These peaks clearly indicate that the samples have a graphitized structure, especially the typical (002) crystal plane[22,23].

The presence of functional groups on the surface of G-CDs and R-CDs particles was then probed by Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopy (Fig. 1c). In the FTIR spectra of G-CDs, the stretching vibration of C-H peaks at 2928 and 2863 cm−1 [24]. A broad absorption region from 1620.9 cm−1 to 1512.2 cm−1 was assigned to the stretching vibration of CO[25]. The absorption band due to the stretching vibration of C-N was located at 1307.0 cm−1 [26]. The sharp peak at 817.5 cm−1 corresponded to the bending vibration of C-H[27]. In the spectra of R-CDs, the telescopic vibrations of N-H and C-H were peaked at 3312.6, 2972 and 2880 cm−1 [28]. Peak at 1087.6 and 1045.7 cm−1 indicated anhydride C-O stretching, respectively[29]. The sharp peak at 879 cm−1 corresponds to the bending vibration of C-H. The presence of special groups such as C-H, CO, C-N on the surface of synthesized G-CDs along with the presence of special groups such as N-H, C-H on the surface of synthesized R-CDs in the infrared spectroscopic analysis proves the successful preparation of carbon dots.

In Fig. 1d-k, the full XPS spectra of G-CDs and R-CDs both show three peaks at C1s (284.8 and 285.1 eV), N1s (399.1 eV) and O1s (532.0 and 532.1 eV). The high-resolution C1s spectrum (Fig. 1e and i) showed three distinct peaks, which were O-CO, C-O, and CC/C-C[30]. The N1s peaks at 401.3 and 399.2 eV in Figs. 1f, 401.1 and 399.1 eV in Fig. 1j both showed two peaks, which indicated that N was mainly present in graphitic N, and amido N[31]. O 1 s XPS spectra of R-CDs shown two oxygen states: a double peak for CO (532 eV) and C-O (533.2 eV) is shown in the figure[32].

The results of excitation and emission spectra ( Fig. 2a) showed that G-CDs had a strong emission wave at 515 nm with a maximum excitation wavelength of 460 nm. However, R-CDs (Fig. 2d) had an optimal emission wavelength of 611 nm and an optimal excitation wavelength of 560 nm. The stokes shifts of G-CDs and R-CDs are 55 nm and 51 nm, respectively. The fluorescence spectra of G-CDs and R-CDs in Fig. 2c and f and the International Commission on Electron Emission (CIE) coordinates (0.2668, 0.6137) and (0.5163, 0.4697) also support these findings. In order to deeply investigate its optical properties, we obtained the three-dimensional luminescence spectrum of the two CDs as shown in Figs. 2b and 2e. Then we measured the absolute quantum efficiency and of the prepared carbon dots, and the G-CDs showed an absolute quantum efficiency of up to 51.8 % (Fig. S3), which suggests that G-CDs have better optical properties than CDs obtained by other synthetic routes (Fig. S4). Meanwhile, the results in Fig.S5 demonstrate that G-CDs and R-CDs are resistant to ionic interference and abnormal temperatures.

To assess whether G-CDs and R-CDs are reliably compatible in cells, we performed cytotoxicity assays. In this work, the cytotoxicity of R-CDs and G-CDs were assayed. As can be seen in Figs. S6a and 6b, after the addition of different concentrations of G-CDs and R-CDs (10, 20, 40, 60, 80, 100, 120, and 140 μg/mL), the cell viability was maintained at more than 80 % when compared with HeLa cells without CDs. Subsequently, we used Calcein/PI Cell Viability Assay Kit to detect the live-dead cell ratio of the cells after incubation with 100 μg/mL CDs for 6 h. The results (Fig. S6c) similarly demonstrated that the carbon dots incubation caused the death of only a very minimal number of cells. Therefore, a solution of 100 μg/mL of CDs was selected for staining the cells [33].

To investigate whether G-CDs and R-CDs have potential in cell imaging and organelle imaging, we captured cell images using laser confocal microscopy. HeLa cells were incubated with red and green dyes for mitochondrial tracker and lysosomal tracker, respectively, according to Fig. 3. Surprisingly, although the fluorescent regions of G-CDs and R-CDs were both shown in the cytoplasm, the fluorescent region showed some differences in a more precise dimension. These results indicate that the merged image of G-CDs (Fig. 3a) exhibited significant consistency with Mito-Tracker Red (The Pearson correlation coefficient between G-CDs and Mito-Tracker Red was 0.82), whereas the merged image of R-CDs (Fig. 3b) demonstrates substantial consistency with Lyso-Tracker Green (The Pearson correlation coefficient between R-CDs and Lyso-Tracker Green was 0.87).

Furthermore, we utilized HeLa cells receiving four different inhibitors to explore the mechanism of cellular CDs uptake (relevant results in Fig. S7-10 of the Supplementary Information). The results suggested that that G-CDs enter cells mainly through caveolae-mediated endocytosis and micropinocytosis[34]. However, the internalisation of R-CDs was mainly affected by lipid raft-mediated endocytosis. (Fig. S9 and S10)[35].

Surprisingly, despite the use of the same precursor materials in synthesis, the two types of CDs exhibited distinct targeting behaviors. We then discussed the underlying reasons for this phenomenon. First, we examined the response of the CDs to pH (Fig. 4 a-d). G-CDs displayed higher fluorescence intensity under alkaline conditions, whereas R-CDs showed significantly enhanced fluorescence in acidic conditions, indicating an affinity of R-CDs for acidic environments. Under alkaline conditions, the carboxyl groups on the surface of G-CDs become deprotonated, resulting in a negative surface charge. This distribution of negative charge likely stabilizes the electronic states of the carbon dots, thereby enhancing their fluorescence emission. In contrast, R-CDs exhibited stronger fluorescence under acidic conditions, potentially due to the protonation of surface amino group, which may enhance photoluminescence efficiency by promoting a favorable charge distribution. Lysosomes, characterized by their acidic internal environment with a pH maintained between 4.5 and 5.0, provide an ideal binding site for R-CDs, whose fluorescence is activated in such conditions. This targeting mechanism is consistent with other imaging probes that utilize the acidic nature of lysosomes, such as Lyso-Tracker and Lyso-Sensor, supporting the notion that the response of R-CDs to acidic conditions underlies their lysosome-targeting behavior.

Subsequently, we measured the Zeta potential of the carbon dots, revealing that G-CDs possess a strong positive charge under neutral and acidic conditions ( Fig. 4e). Mitochondria, as the site of reactions associated with the electron transport chain, have a highly negative membrane potential. This negative potential renders the positively charged G-CDs more likely to exhibit higher affinity towards the mitochondrial inner membrane. Additionally, we determined the partition coefficient (Log P) value for G-CDs, which was calculated to be 1.52[36]. The result in Fig. S11 indicates that the concentration of G-CDs in octanol is 33 times higher than in water, demonstrating their high lipophilicity. The hydrophilicity and lipophilicity of small molecules largely determine their intracellular distribution. The good lipophilicity of G-CDs enables them to easily traverse the bilayer membrane structure of mitochondria. Moreover, endocytosis is highly correlated with lysosomal activity. Cells internalize external substances through the formation of endocytic vesicles, which subsequently fuse with lysosomes in the cytoplasm[37,38]. This suggests that larger R-CDs may tend to remain within the lysosomes, whereas smaller G-CDs are more likely to escape from lysosomes.

Since G-CDs show excellent ability to target mitochondria for imaging, we developed applications to use G-CDs for intracellular mitochondrial physiological state monitoring. To investigate the targeting mechanism of our G-CDs, cells were treated with CCCP, a typical reagent that reduces the MMP of mitochondria. Afterwards, HeLa cells were stained with the membrane potential red probe TMRE and G-CDs. The results showed that CCCP treatment diffused green fluorescence to the area of the cell nucleus. In addition, compared with the untreated group, showed the same change of attenuation of fluorescence intensity as the red probe (the relevant results were demonstrated in Fig. 5 and S12). The above results suggest that the targeting of G-CDs to mitochondria is dependent on the negative membrane potential of mitochondria, and that G-CDs bind to mitochondria mainly through electrostatic interactions[39]. In addition, according to the correlation results in Fig. S13, G-CDs showed better fluorescence stability than TMRE. After 60 s of high-intensity laser irradiation, the fluorescence of TMRE in the field of view was basically completely burst, but the G-CDs were also visible with obvious mitochondrial structure.

In pathologic conditions, the morphology and function of mitochondria are altered from their normal state. Mitochondrial autophagy, known as mitophagy, is a crucial biological process within cells, playing a vital role in maintaining the dynamic balance of cell populations. Thus, we focused on investigating the dynamic changes of mitochondria during autophagy, particularly before and after treatment with rapamycin, as depicted in Fig. 6. Initially, HeLa cells were stained with G-CDs, followed by treatment with 50 μg/mL of rapamycin. Then, confocal images were acquired at 0–20 min. As shown in Fig. 6, after 10 minutes, there was a decrease in fluorescence intensity, and mitochondrial filamentous structures began to fragment, indicating ongoing mitophagy. Furthermore, twenty minutes after rapamycin was injected, 20 minutes after the addition of rapamycin, mitochondrial well-defined structures transitioned dots diffused in the cell, accompanied by a reduction in fluorescence intensity compared to earlier time points, possibly due to changes induced by rapamycin in the cellular microenvironment[40].

Due to the potential value of R-CDs for targeting lysosomes. We explored the applicability of R-CDs probes for tracking lysosomes in real cellular physiology, and therefore we performed live cellular lysosome tracking experiments. Lysosomes are organelles distributed in the cytoplasmic region with highly dynamic characteristics. Chloroquine was selected to treat cells, and this drug inhibited the process of fusion of autophagosomes with lysosomes. Chloroquine was able to alter the acidic environment of lysosomes, causing lysosomes to move in the cell within a short time frame. 5 mM chloroquine was added to HeLa cells that had been stained by R-CDs in advance, and images were acquired at 0, 4, 8, 12, and 16 min ( Fig. 7a). In order to clearly show the trend of lysosomal movement, the images at different times were combined and the direction of lysosomal movement was labeled with arrows in the small figure. The above results show that R-CDs can monitor the dynamic changes of lysosomes in cells[41].

We also used 0.5 % NaOH to induce cell necrosis, as shown in Fig. 7b. It can be observed that the fluorescent regions gradually change over time. Eventually, the necrotic process is accompanied by the release of cellular contents from the nucleus. Lysosomes not only accumulatee in the cytoplasm but also display a tendency to aggregate towards the nuclear region to degrade substances released as a result of nuclear damage. This is consistent with the dynamic changes of lysosomes during the process of cell necrosis. Ultimately, the result of cell necrosis leads to the release of lysosomal contents, including the escape of R-CDs from the lysosome, which are dispersed in the cytoplasm[42]. Based on these results, R-CDs targeting lysosomes can serve as an effective tool for accurately tracking the physiological processes of lysosome-related cellular activities.

To extend the imaging application of carbon dots, we used G-CDs and R-CDs for imaging of organoids. G-CDs and Mito-Tracker Red were co-stained for intestinal organoids, and the results are shown in the Fig. 8a-e. In addition, R-CDs and Lyso-Tracker Green were co-stained against intestinal organoids, and the results of the confocal images are shown in Fig. 8f-j. The results showed that the fluorescent areas of G-CDs and R-CDs were mainly distributed in the cytoplasmic portion, and there was a large overlap with the stained areas of Mito-Tracker and Lyso-Tracker. The G-CDs, R-CDs and Dapi were then incubated against the intestinal organoids, and the three-dimensional fluorescence images are shown in Fig. 8k-n. We found that in the application of organoid imaging, long-wavelength fluorescence-emitting carbon dots would have better imaging effect compared to short-wavelength fluorescence-emitting carbon dots. These suggested to us that the development of long-wavelength fluorescence-emitting carbon dots is necessary for imaging applications, such as imaging of tissues, organoid and organs.

Fluorescence images of zebrafish larvae and eggs were acquired under a 10× objective of a confocal laser scanning microscope. By merging the bright-field and fluorescence field images, the distribution of both types of carbon dots in zebrafish larvae and eggs can be observed. The results in Fig. 9 indicate that G-CDs and R-CDs not only accumulate in the abdomen of zebrafish but also circulate through the bloodstream to the spinal cord, brain, and eyes. These findings demonstrate that G-CDs and R-CDs possess favorable properties for crossing vascular barriers, suggesting their potential value in the field of zebrafish imaging.

In conclusion, we obtained 525 nm fluorescence-emitting green fluorescent carbon dots by hydrothermal method using 1,5-diaminoanthraquinone, vanillic acid and ethylenediamine as precursors; and 611 nm fluorescence-emitting red fluorescent carbon dots by ethanol solvent thermophoresis. The G-CDs have mitochondrial targeting ability due to their higher positive surface charge and good lipophilicity. The R-CDs have affinity for acidic organelle lysosomes due to showing stronger fluorescence intensity under weakly alkaline conditions. Based on the unique properties of these carbon dots, we utilized G-CDs to observe mitochondrial changes in HeLa cells during apoptosis and mitochondrial autophagy. Meanwhile, the dynamics of lysosomes in the inhibition of cellular autophagy and cell necrosis were observed by utilizing R-CDs. In addition, we applied G-CDs and R-CDs to zebrafish and intestinal cancer-like organs for carbon spot imaging. To summarize, we extended G-CDs and R-CDs to multiple potential applications including organelles, zebrafish, and organoids, which provide new perspectives for studies related to bioimaging of carbon dots.

Ruhong Yan: Project administration. Wenfei Dong: Supervision, Conceptualization. Li Li: Writing – review & editing, Supervision. Xinlu Li: Writing – original draft, Methodology. Tongtong Zhu: Visualization. Xiangli Quan: Visualization. Haiyang Yan: Validation. Yuwei Du: Software.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was funded by the National Key R&D Program of China (2023YFB3210300), the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 82172077, 22201298 and 62027825), the Science Foundation of the Chinese Academy of Sciences (No. 2023SYHZ0037), the Natural Science Foundation of Jiangsu Province of China (BK20231207), the Innovation and Entrepreneurship Team of Jiangsu Province (JSSCTD202145), The Projects of Chinese Academy of Sciences (YJKYYQ20200038 and YJKYYQ20210032), and the Youth Innovation Promotion Association of CAS (2022326).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.snb.2024.136678.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: The role of lysosome in regulated necrosis

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: A. Alu, X. Han, X. Ma, M. Wu, Y. Wei, X. Wei, The role of lysosome in regulated necrosis, Acta Pharmaceutica Sinica B 10(10) (2020) 1880-1903.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Xinlu Li is a Master's student at the school of Biomedical Engineering (Suzhou), University of Science and Technology of China. majoring in Biological and Medical Sciences. Her research focuses on the development of fluorescent probes.

Tongtong Zhu is a Doctoral student at the school of Biomedical Engineering (Suzhou), University of Science and Technology of China. majoring in Biomedical Engineering. His research focuses on the applications of fluorescent probes in multimodal imaging.

Haiyang Yan is a Doctoral student at the school of Biomedical Engineering (Suzhou), University of Science and Technology of China, majoring in Electronic and Information Engineering. His research focuses on organ-on-a-chip technology.

Yuwei Du is a Doctoral student at the school of Biomedical Engineering (Suzhou), University of Science and Technology of China. majoring in Biomedical Engineering. Her research focuses on the microfluidic applications of probes.
Ruhong Yan is a Doctor of Medicine. Her primary research focuses on the clinical detection and study of tumors.
Wen-Fei Dong is a professor at the Suzhou Institute of Biomedical Engineering and Technology, Chinese Academy of Sciences. He has long been engaged in the research of nanobiomedical photonics.

Li Li is a professor at the Suzhou Institute of Biomedical Engineering and Technology, Chinese Academy of Sciences. He has long been engaged in the biological applications of high-performance fluorescent probes.
